is a [software framework](/wiki/Software_framework "Software framework") primarily designed for [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") development, which may include specialized [software libraries](/wiki/Library_(computing) "Library (computing)") and packages, such as [level editors](/wiki/Level_editors "Level editors").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The term "engine" is a direct analogue of "[software engine](/wiki/Software_engine "Software engine")", having it employed across sectors of the [software industry](/wiki/Software_industry "Software industry").

A **game engine** is a [software framework](/wiki/Software_framework "Software framework") primarily designed for [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") development, which may include specialized [software libraries](/wiki/Library_(computing) "Library (computing)") and packages, such as [level editors](/wiki/Level_editors "Level editors").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The term "engine" is a direct analogue of "[software engine](/wiki/Software_engine "Software engine")", having it employed across sectors of the [software industry](/wiki/Software_industry "Software industry").

Furthermore, *game engine* may also refer to the integrated development environment as an interface behind the given framework, typically a suite of visual development tools and features for developing video games.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Game developers](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer") can use game engines to create and publish video games across platforms, such as [desktops](/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer"), [mobile devices](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device"), [video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console"), and other types of [computers](/wiki/Computer "Computer"). The core functionalities commonly covered by a game engine are a [2D](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics") or [3D](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") [renderer](/wiki/Rendering_(computer_graphics) "Rendering (computer graphics)"), [physics engine](/wiki/Physics_engine "Physics engine"), [audio engine](/wiki/Audio_engine "Audio engine"), [scripting](/wiki/Scripting_language "Scripting language"), [animation](/wiki/Computer_animation "Computer animation"), [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Game_AI "Game AI"), [networking](/wiki/Computer_networking "Computer networking"), streaming, [memory management](/wiki/Memory_management "Memory management"), [threads](/wiki/Thread_(computing) "Thread (computing)"), [localization](/wiki/Internationalization_and_localization "Internationalization and localization") support, [scene graph](/wiki/Scene_graph "Scene graph"), and [cinematics](/wiki/Cinematic_cutscene "Cinematic cutscene"). Game engine developers often economize [game development](/wiki/Game_development "Game development") through reuse or adaptation of abstractions built into game engines for production of various games,[[4]](#cite_note-4) or through [porting](/wiki/Porting "Porting") of videogames from a single to other target platforms.

## Purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Game_engine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Purpose")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/UI_of_Godot_Game_Engine.jpg/330px-UI_of_Godot_Game_Engine.jpg)](/wiki/File:UI_of_Godot_Game_Engine.jpg)

Creating a [platform game](/wiki/Platform_game "Platform game") in [Godot](/wiki/Godot_(game_engine) "Godot (game engine)")

In many cases, game engines provide a suite of visual [development tools](/wiki/Game_development_tool "Game development tool") in addition to reusable software components. These tools are generally provided in an [integrated development environment](/wiki/Integrated_development_environment "Integrated development environment") to enable simplified, [rapid development](/wiki/Rapid_application_development "Rapid application development") of games in a [data-driven](/wiki/Data-driven_design "Data-driven design") manner. Game-engine developers often attempt to preempt implementer needs by developing [robust](/wiki/Robustness_(computer_science) "Robustness (computer science)") software suites, which include many elements a game developer may need to build a game. Most game-engine suites provide facilities that ease development, such as graphics, sound, physics and [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") (AI) functions. These game engines are sometimes called "[middleware](/wiki/Middleware "Middleware")" because, as with the business sense of the term, they provide a flexible and reusable software platform which provides all the core functionality needed, right [out of the box](/wiki/Out_of_the_box_(feature) "Out of the box (feature)"), to develop a game application while reducing costs, complexities, and time-to-market—all critical factors in the highly competitive video game industry.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Like other types of middleware, game engines usually provide [platform](/wiki/Computing_platform "Computing platform") [abstraction](/wiki/Abstraction_(computer_science) "Abstraction (computer science)"), allowing the same game to run on various platforms (including [game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") and personal computers) with few, if any, changes made to the game [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code"). Often, programmers design game engines with a [component-based architecture](/wiki/Component-based_software_engineering "Component-based software engineering") that allows specific systems in the engine to be replaced or extended with more specialized (and often more expensive) game-middleware components. Some game engines comprise a series of loosely-connected game middleware components that can be selectively combined to create a custom engine, instead of the more common approach of extending or customizing a flexible integrated product. However achieved, [extensibility](/wiki/Extensibility "Extensibility") remains a high priority for game engines due to the wide variety of uses for which they are applied. Despite the specificity of the name "game engine", end users often re-purpose game engines for other kinds of interactive applications with [real-time graphical](/wiki/Real-time_computer_graphics "Real-time computer graphics") requirements—such as [marketing demos](/wiki/Product_demonstration "Product demonstration"), [architectural visualizations](/wiki/Architectural_visualization "Architectural visualization"), [training simulations](/wiki/Training_simulation "Training simulation"), and [modeling](/wiki/Conceptual_model "Conceptual model") environments.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)

Some game engines only provide [real-time 3D rendering](/wiki/Real-time_computer_graphics "Real-time computer graphics") capabilities instead of the wide range of functionality needed by games. These engines rely upon the game developer to implement the rest of this functionality or to assemble it from other game-middleware components. These types of engines are generally referred to as a "graphics engine", "rendering engine", or "3D engine" instead of the all-encompassing term "game engine". This terminology is inconsistently used, as many full-featured 3D game engines are simply referred to as "3D engines". Examples of graphics engines include: [Crystal Space](/wiki/Crystal_Space "Crystal Space"), [Genesis3D](/wiki/Genesis3D "Genesis3D"), [Irrlicht](/wiki/Irrlicht_Engine "Irrlicht Engine"), [OGRE](/wiki/OGRE "OGRE"), RealmForge, Truevision3D, and [Vision Engine](/wiki/Vision_Engine "Vision Engine"). Modern game- or graphics-engines generally provide a [scene graph](/wiki/Scene_graph "Scene graph")—an object-oriented representation of the 3D game-world which often simplifies game design and can be used for more efficient rendering of vast virtual worlds.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Most game engines or graphics engines use a [Graphics API](/wiki/Graphics_API "Graphics API"), which eases communication with the [GPU](/wiki/GPU "GPU"). But older games did not have hardware acceleration or GPUs and had to build their own software renderer.

As technology ages, the components of an engine may become obsolete or insufficient for the requirements of a given [project](/wiki/Project "Project"). Since the complexity of programming an entirely new engine may result in unwanted delays (or necessitate that a project restart from the beginning), an engine-development team may elect to update their existing engine with newer functionality or components.

Game engines are not limited to use in the video game field, and have found uses in other scientific fields. Part of this is due to game engines being optimized for consumer-grade computing equipment rather than high-performance computing, allowing researchers to develop programs for their use on lower cost machines. Game engines also simplify many of the computing operations needed for scientific software, including graphics, networking, and interactivity, reducing the need to develop these features themselves.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Game_engine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Quake_-_family_tree_2_Simplified.svg/330px-Quake_-_family_tree_2_Simplified.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Quake_-_family_tree_2_Simplified.svg)

Some game engines experience an evolution over time and develop a family tree, like for instance [id's](/wiki/Id_software "Id software") [Quake engine](/wiki/Quake_engine "Quake engine") which resulted in the [id Tech](/wiki/Id_Tech "Id Tech") family.

Before game engines, games were typically written as singular entities: a game for the [Atari 2600](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600"), for example, had to be designed from the bottom up to make optimal use of the display hardware—this core display routine is today called the [kernel](/wiki/Kernel_(operating_system) "Kernel (operating system)") by developers of games for older systems. Other platforms had more leeway, but even when the display was not a concern, memory constraints usually sabotaged attempts to create the data-heavy design that an engine needs. Even on more accommodating platforms, very little could be reused between games. The rapid advance of [arcade](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") [hardware](/wiki/Arcade_system_board "Arcade system board")—which was the leading edge of the market at the time—meant that most of the code would have to be thrown out afterwards anyway, as later generations of games would use completely different game designs that took advantage of extra resources. Thus, most game designs through the 1980s were designed through a hard-coded rule set with a small number of levels and graphics data. Since the [golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games"), it became common for video game companies to develop in-house game engines for use with first-party software.

A notable example of an in-house game engine on [home consoles](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console") in the mid-1980s was the smooth [side-scrolling](/wiki/Side-scrolling "Side-scrolling") engine developed by [Shigeru Miyamoto](/wiki/Shigeru_Miyamoto "Shigeru Miyamoto")'s team at [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") for the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") (NES). The engine they had developed for the side-scrolling [racing game](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game") *[Excitebike](/wiki/Excitebike "Excitebike")* (1984) was later employed for the scrolling [platformer](/wiki/Platformer "Platformer") *[Super Mario Bros.](/wiki/Super_Mario_Bros. "Super Mario Bros.")* (1985). This allowed [Mario](/wiki/Mario "Mario") to smoothly accelerate from a walk to a run, rather than move at a constant speed like in earlier platformers.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

While third-party game engines were not common until the rise of [3D computer graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") in the 1990s, several 2D [game creation systems](/wiki/Game_creation_system "Game creation system") were produced in the 1980s for [independent video game development](/wiki/Independent_video_game_development "Independent video game development"). These include *[Pinball Construction Set](/wiki/Pinball_Construction_Set "Pinball Construction Set")* (1983), [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII_(company) "ASCII (company)")'s *War Game Construction Kit* (1983),[[10]](#cite_note-10) *[Thunder Force Construction](/wiki/Thunder_Force_(series) "Thunder Force (series)")* (1984),[[11]](#cite_note-11) *[Adventure Construction Set](/wiki/Adventure_Construction_Set "Adventure Construction Set")* (1984), *[Garry Kitchen's GameMaker](/wiki/Garry_Kitchen%27s_GameMaker "Garry Kitchen's GameMaker")* (1985), *[Wargame Construction Set](/wiki/Wargame_Construction_Set "Wargame Construction Set")* (1986), *[Shoot-'Em-Up Construction Kit](/wiki/Shoot-%27Em-Up_Construction_Kit "Shoot-'Em-Up Construction Kit")* (1987), *[Arcade Game Construction Kit](/wiki/Arcade_Game_Construction_Kit "Arcade Game Construction Kit")* (1988), and, most popularly, ASCII's *[RPG Maker](/wiki/RPG_Maker "RPG Maker")* engines from 1998 onward. Klik & Play (1994) is another legacy offering that is still available.[[12]](#cite_note-produsase_japan-12)

The term *game engine* emerged in the mid-1990s, particularly with the rise of 3D games like [first-person shooters](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter"), which often featured a dedicated [first-person shooter engine](/wiki/First-person_shooter_engine "First-person shooter engine"). For instance, Epic Games, founded by [Tim Sweeney](/wiki/Tim_Sweeney "Tim Sweeney"), debuted its Unreal Engine in 1998.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Such was the popularity of [Id Software](/wiki/Id_Software "Id Software")'s *[Doom](/wiki/Doom_(1993_video_game) "Doom (1993 video game)")* and *[Quake](/wiki/Quake_(series) "Quake (series)")* games: rather than building from scratch, its developers licensed the core portions of the software and designed their own graphics, characters, weapons, and [levels](/wiki/Level_(computer_and_video_games) "Level (computer and video games)")—they were the "game content" or "game assets." Separation of game-specific rules and data from basic concepts like [collision detection](/wiki/Collision_detection "Collision detection") and game entity meant that teams could grow and specialize.[[12]](#cite_note-produsase_japan-12)

Later games, such as [id Software](/wiki/Id_Software "Id Software")'s *[Quake III Arena](/wiki/Quake_III_Arena "Quake III Arena")* and [Epic Games](/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games")' *[Unreal](/wiki/Unreal_(1998_video_game) "Unreal (1998 video game)")* (1998), were designed with this approach in mind, with the engine and content developed separately. The practice of licensing such technology has proved to be a useful auxiliary revenue stream for some game developers, as a single license for a high-end commercial game engine can range from $10,000 to millions of dollars, and the number of licensees can reach several dozen companies, as seen with the [Unreal Engine](/wiki/Unreal_Engine "Unreal Engine"). At the very least, reusable engines make developing game sequels faster and easier, which is a valuable advantage in the competitive video game industry. While a strong rivalry existed between Epic Games and id Software around 2000, Epic Game's Unreal Engine has since become far more popular than [id Tech 4](/wiki/Id_Tech_4 "Id Tech 4") and its successor, [id Tech 5](/wiki/Id_Tech_5 "Id Tech 5").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Modern game engines are some of the most complex applications written, often featuring dozens of finely-tuned systems interacting to ensure a precisely-controlled user experience. The continued evolution of game engines has created a strong separation between rendering, scripting, artwork, and [level design](/wiki/Level_design "Level design"). As such, it is now common, for example, for a typical game development team to have several times as many artists as actual programmers.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

While third-party game engines are predominantly used in the development of first-person shooter games, they are also being used across other genres. For example, [Gamebryo](/wiki/Gamebryo "Gamebryo") is used in *[The Elder Scrolls III: Morrowind](/wiki/The_Elder_Scrolls_III:_Morrowind "The Elder Scrolls III: Morrowind")* and *[Dark Age of Camelot](/wiki/Dark_Age_of_Camelot "Dark Age of Camelot")*, whereas [Unreal Engine 2](/wiki/Unreal_Engine_2 "Unreal Engine 2") is used in *[Lineage II](/wiki/Lineage_II "Lineage II")*. Game engines are also used for games originally developed for home consoles, with [RenderWare](/wiki/RenderWare "RenderWare") for the *[Grand Theft Auto](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto "Grand Theft Auto")* and *[Burnout](/wiki/Burnout_(game) "Burnout (game)")* franchises as an example.

Due to modern multi-core systems and growing demands in realism, exploiting [threads](/wiki/Thread_(computer_science) "Thread (computer science)") has become more important. Separate threads of execution within a game engine manage intensive operations, including rendering, asset streaming, audio playback, and physics simulation. Racing games have typically been at the forefront of threading with the physics engine running in a separate thread long before other core subsystems were moved, partly because rendering and related tasks need updating at only 30–60 Hz. For example, on PlayStation 3, physics ran in *[Need For Speed](/wiki/Need_For_Speed "Need For Speed")* at 100 Hz, versus *[Forza Motorsport 2](/wiki/Forza_Motorsport_2 "Forza Motorsport 2")* at 360 Hz.

Although the term was first used in the 1990s, few earlier systems in 1980s are considered as game engines, such as Sierra's [Adventure Game Interpreter](/wiki/Adventure_Game_Interpreter "Adventure Game Interpreter") (AGI) and SCI systems, LucasArts' [SCUMM](/wiki/SCUMM "SCUMM") system and [Incentive Software](/wiki/Incentive_Software "Incentive Software")'s [Freescape engine](/wiki/Freescape_engine "Freescape engine") (in 1986[[16]](#cite_note-16)). Unlike most modern game engines, these game engines were never used in any third-party products (except for the SCUMM system, which was licensed to and used by [Humongous Entertainment](/wiki/Humongous_Entertainment "Humongous Entertainment")).

As game engine technology matures and becomes more user friendly, the application of game engines has widened in scope. They are now used for [serious games](/wiki/Serious_game "Serious game"): visualization, training, medical, and [military simulation](/wiki/Military_simulation "Military simulation") applications, with the [CryEngine](/wiki/CryEngine "CryEngine") being one example.[[17]](#cite_note-17) To improve accessibility, new hardware platforms are also now targeted by game engines, including [mobile phones](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") (e.g. [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"), [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone")) and [web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"). (e.g. [WebGL](/wiki/WebGL "WebGL"), [Shockwave](/wiki/Adobe_Shockwave "Adobe Shockwave"), [Flash](/wiki/Adobe_Flash "Adobe Flash"), [Trinigy](/wiki/Trinigy "Trinigy")'s WebVision, [Silverlight](/wiki/Silverlight "Silverlight"), [Unity Web Player](/wiki/Unity_Web_Player "Unity Web Player"), [O3D](/wiki/O3D "O3D") and pure [DHTML](/wiki/DHTML "DHTML").)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Additionally, more game engines are being built upon [higher-level languages](/wiki/High-level_programming_language "High-level programming language"), such as [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)"), [C#](/wiki/C_Sharp_(programming_language) "C Sharp (programming language)") and [.NET](/wiki/.NET_Framework ".NET Framework") (e.g., [TorqueX](/wiki/Torque_Game_Engine "Torque Game Engine"), and [Visual3D.NET](/wiki/Visual3D.NET "Visual3D.NET")), [Python](/wiki/Python_(programming_language) "Python (programming language)") ([Panda3D](/wiki/Panda3D "Panda3D")), or [Lua Script](/wiki/Lua_(programming_language) "Lua (programming language)") (Leadwerks). As most 3D-rich games are now mostly limited by the power of a graphics card, the potential slowdown due to translation overheads of higher-level languages becomes negligible, while productivity gains offered by these languages serve the game engine developers' benefit.[[19]](#cite_note-19) These recent trends are being propelled by companies such as [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") to support [indie](/wiki/Independent_game_development "Independent game development") game development. Microsoft developed [XNA](/wiki/Microsoft_XNA "Microsoft XNA") as the primary [Software Development Kit](/wiki/Software_development_kit "Software development kit") (SDK) for all video games released on Xbox and their related products. This includes the [Xbox Live Indie Games](/wiki/Xbox_Live_Indie_Games "Xbox Live Indie Games")[[20]](#cite_note-20) channel designed specifically for smaller developers who do not have the extensive resources necessary to box games for sale on retail shelves. It is becoming easier and cheaper than ever to develop game engines for platforms that support [managed](/wiki/Managed_code "Managed code") frameworks.[[21]](#cite_note-:0-21)

## Game engines as an industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Game_engine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Game engines as an industry")]

Producers of game engines decide how they allow users to use their products. Just as [gaming is an industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry"), so are the engines they are built off. The major game engines come at varying prices, whether it be in the form of subscription fees or license payments.[[22]](#cite_note-22) [Unity](/wiki/Unity_(game_engine) "Unity (game engine)") and [Unreal Engine](/wiki/Unreal_Engine "Unreal Engine") are currently the two most popular choices for game developers.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Although the differences among the different game engines blur as they build their own tools on top of them, different game developers may be too used to a system to change, or attracted by the huge benefits of such engines regardless of pay-walls.

[Free and open-source](/wiki/Free_and_open-source_software "Free and open-source software") engines such as [Godot](/wiki/Godot_(game_engine) "Godot (game engine)"), have seen a rise in popularity, particularly following the [2023 Unity pricing controversy](/wiki/Unity_Technologies#Pricing_controversy "Unity Technologies") which drove game developers and studios to seek engines with a permissive license.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Game middleware

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Game_engine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Game middleware")]

Applied more broadly, game engines themselves can be described as middleware. In video games, however, *middleware* is often used to refer to subsystems of functionality within a game engine. Some game middleware does only one thing, yet does it more convincingly or more efficiently than general purpose middleware.

The four most-widely used middleware packages[[25]](#cite_note-25) that provide subsystems of functionality include [RAD Game Tools](/wiki/RAD_Game_Tools "RAD Game Tools")' Bink, Firelight [FMOD](/wiki/FMOD "FMOD"), [Havok](/wiki/Havok_(software) "Havok (software)"), and [Scaleform](/wiki/Scaleform "Scaleform") GFx. RAD Game Tools develops Bink for basic video rendering, along with Miles audio, and [Granny 3D](/wiki/Granny_3D "Granny 3D") rendering. Firelight FMOD is a low cost robust audio library and toolset. Havok provides a robust physics simulation system, along with a suite of animation and behavior applications. Scaleform provides GFx for high performance [Flash](/wiki/Adobe_Flash "Adobe Flash") UI and high-quality video playback, and an [Input Method Editor](/wiki/Input_Method_Editor "Input Method Editor") (IME) addon for in-game Asian chat support.

Other middleware is used for performance optimization—for example [Simplygon](/wiki/Simplygon "Simplygon") helps to optimize and generate [level of detail](/wiki/Level_of_detail_(computer_graphics) "Level of detail (computer graphics)") meshes, and [Umbra](/wiki/Umbra_(3D_technology_company) "Umbra (3D technology company)") adds [occlusion culling](/wiki/Occlusion_culling "Occlusion culling") optimizations to 3D graphics.

Some middleware contains full [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code"),[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) or is provided an [API](/wiki/Application_programming_interface "Application programming interface") reference for a compiled binary [library](/wiki/Library_(computer_science) "Library (computer science)"). Some middleware programs can be licensed either way, usually for a higher fee for full source code.
